name: The 3-2-1 Trick
summary: Stop rambling. Force your brain into a clean numbered structure that audiences can follow instantly.
usage_scenario: Best for Q&A sessions, impromptu speeches, and social media content.
steps: Pick 3 main points, types, or reasons
Narrow it to 2 supporting ideas
Land on 1 core takeaway

name: P.R.E.P Method
summary: The gold standard for professional, structured opinions. Delivers your view in four clear steps.
usage_scenario: Ideal for job interviews, business meetings, and debate-style questions.
steps: Point — state your clear position
Reason — explain why you believe it
Example — support with a real story or data
Point — restate your position to close

name: P.A.S Framework
summary: Pull your audience in emotionally by identifying a problem they feel deeply, then offering your solution.
usage_scenario: Perfect for persuasive speeches, sales pitches, and change-of-mind arguments.
steps: Problem — name the pain point clearly
Agitation — intensify the emotion around it
Solution — introduce your answer as the relief

name: H.I.C Framework
summary: Give any talk a crisp, three-part arc — grab them, inform them, then move them to act.
usage_scenario: Great for presentations, class reports, and short elevator pitches.
steps: Hook — open with a story, question, or bold statement
Inform — deliver your key message and evidence
Call to Action — close by telling the audience exactly what to do next

name: S.T.A.R Method
summary: Tell powerful, memorable stories by walking through a real-life experience step by step.
usage_scenario: Best for behavioural interview questions ('Tell me about a time when...') and personal narratives.
steps: Situation — set the scene and give context
Task — describe your specific responsibility
Action — explain the steps you personally took
Result — share the measurable outcome

name: A.I.D.A Framework
summary: Classic marketing and persuasion arc that moves listeners from awareness to action.
usage_scenario: Excellent for pitches, product demos, and motivational talks.
steps: Attention — capture focus with a bold opener
Interest — build curiosity with relevant facts
Desire — ignite want by showing personal benefit
Action — give a clear, simple next step
